summary: Introduces 'hacer' (to do/make), 'puedo/puedes' (I can/you can), 'comprar' (to buy), 'llegar' (to arrive), 'esperar' (to wait/hope), and 'salir' (to leave). Recaps the -s for informal you and -n for they pattern across both verb groups. Emphasizes checking the infinitive ending before conjugating.

teachings.summary: Hacer means 'to do' or 'to make'. It's an -er verb with silent h. Conjugates regularly for he/she/it (hace), they (hacen), you informal (haces), but irregular for I (hago, covered later).

examples.english: What are you doing? (also: how are you?)

teachings.notes: ¿Qué haces? is a casual way to say 'how are you?' / 'what are you up to?'

teachings.summary: Across all verb types, -s marks informal you and -n marks they/you(plural). These associations persist throughout all Spanish verb tenses.
teachings.rule: -s = you (informal), -n = they/you (plural). This applies across all tenses.

teachings.summary: Puedo means 'I can' or 'I am able'. It's an -er verb. Puede = he/she/it can, puedes = you (informal) can. Related to English 'power'.
teachings.rule: Puedo (I can), puede (he/she can), puedes (you informal can). Like 'I am able to...' — takes infinitive directly, no 'a'.

examples.spanish: Quiero saber por qué no puedes venir
examples.english: I want to know why you can't come

teachings.notes: Related to 'power'. The d in puedo is quite soft in Spanish.

teachings.conceptName: Always check the infinitive ending before conjugating
teachings.summary: Before conjugating any verb, take one split-second to identify: is it -ar, -er, or -ir? Otherwise your brain may use vowel harmony (putting an e because the stem has e sounds) and produce wrong forms.
teachings.rule: Always go back to the infinitive form before conjugating. Esperar is -ar, so he waits = espera (not espere).
teachings.notes: The brain naturally wants vowel harmony (espe- wants an -e ending). Turkish formalizes this as vowel harmony. Go back to the infinitive to fight this.

teachings.summary: Esperar means both 'to wait' and 'to hope'. Related to English 'desperate' (des- = un-, so hopeless). No need for the word 'for' — just say 'they wait me'.
teachings.rule: Esperar doesn't need a preposition for its object: 'me esperan' = they wait for me.

examples.spanish: Quiero saber por qué no me esperan
examples.english: I want to know why they're not waiting for me

teachings.summary: Salir means 'to leave' or 'to go out'. Irregular I form: salgo (same -go pattern as tengo, vengo). Regular for other forms. It's a verb of movement, takes 'a'.
teachings.rule: Salgo (I leave, irregular -go). Verb of movement: salgo a comer = I'm going out to eat.

teachings.summary: English words ending -ary become -ario in Spanish.

There's a difference between memorization (active, forced, borrowed) and remembering (natural, through practical application). The method avoids memorization but relies on internalization through constructing sentences.
teachings.notes: You borrow information when you memorize it. When you apply it practically in sentence construction, you internalize it — then it's yours.
